screens.name: Lead-sheet songs workspace
screens.purpose: Help a solo player scan a chart library and open one copied lead sheet with enough structure to play from it.
screens.primary_action: Select a chart from the library

ux_rules: Keep the list-detail workspace pattern so the song library remains visible while the chart is open.
Treat the digital chart as the dominant surface and keep framing copy secondary.
Use chart-centric metadata in both list and detail views, prioritizing key, feel, meter, and structure over descriptive prose.
Render each lead-sheet system with stable horizontal measure rhythm and enough whitespace to scan chord changes quickly.
Attach repeats, meter changes, and ending directions to the specific system where they matter rather than hiding them in summary text.
Preserve a paper-like editorial tone with restrained surfaces and subtle score-line cues instead of generic cards.

screen_contexts.layout_intent: Use a persistent chart list on the left and a paper-like lead-sheet reading pane on the right.
interaction_risks: The chart can drift back into plain text blocks if systems and measures are not visually grouped.
Excessive card chrome can make the score feel like a dashboard instead of a practical chart.
System directives may become detached from their rows if they are only shown in the page header.
Too much prose in the list can hide the structural differences between charts.
notes: The viewer should approximate the logic of handwritten staff systems without attempting literal notation engraving.
The provided handwritten sample should drive at least one seed chart so the design proves real repeat and directive cases.

constraints: Model systems and measures explicitly instead of storing every chart row as an opaque string.
Keep the viewer accessible and readable on mobile by allowing horizontal scroll inside systems rather than shrinking chord labels too far.
Use subtle score-line background treatment and borders to imply staff systems without creating noisy decoration.
Do not add competing action buttons inside the chart library or chart pane.
open_decisions: Future support for codas, first and second endings, and alternate takes remains deferred beyond the current system shape.
Search and filters are still deferred until the library grows.

critical_judgments: The viewer should read like a playable chart rather than a generic content detail page.
The selected chart should remain obvious in the list with minimal visual noise.
System-level directives should be easy to find without sacrificing overall calm.

visual_constraints: Favor warm paper tones, graphite-like text, and measured border contrast.
Keep corners restrained and avoid inflated pills or glossy callouts.
Use typography hierarchy and staff-line rhythm as the primary source of structure.
